name: Arjuna Full
description: Arjuna, also known as Partha and Dhananjaya, is a central figure in the Hindu epic Mahabharata. He is the third of the Pandava brothers, born to Kunti and Indra, the king of the gods. Renowned for his unparalleled archery skills, unwavering devotion to dharma, and profound spiritual understanding, Arjuna embodies the ideal warrior-philosopher. He is a skilled warrior, strategist, and devoted disciple of Drona and later, Krishna. Arjuna's journey is marked by trials of strength, courage, and moral dilemmas. His most significant moment occurs on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, where, faced with the prospect of fighting his own kin, he experiences a crisis of faith. Krishna, as his charioteer and divine guide, reveals the Bhagavad Gita to him, imparting profound wisdom on duty, devotion, and the nature of reality. Arjuna's willingness to learn and his ultimate embrace of his dharma solidify his status as a timeless hero, representing the struggle between good and evil, the importance of righteous action, and the path to spiritual enlightenment. He is considered one of the greatest warriors of his time and a symbol of unwavering dedication and selflessness.
subtitle: The Paragon of Archery and Dharma
achievements: Obtaining the divine weapons from Indra

keyMyths.title: Arjuna's Penance
keyMyths.description: Arjuna undertakes a severe penance to obtain divine weapons from Shiva, demonstrating his dedication and strength. This highlights his willingness to endure hardship for the sake of dharma and the Pandava cause.

keyMyths.title: The Bhagavad Gita
keyMyths.description: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna reveals the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna, providing profound philosophical insights. This dialogue explores the nature of duty, devotion, and the ultimate reality, shaping Arjuna's understanding of dharma.

keyMyths.title: Winning Draupadi's Hand
keyMyths.description: Arjuna successfully completes a challenging archery task at Draupadi's swayamvara, proving his skill and winning her hand in marriage. This event solidifies the Pandavas' alliance and sets the stage for future conflicts.
